Lesson 1 of 5 · 50 Minutes
Planning the Investigation
Title · Aim · Hypothesis · Variables · Risk · Materials
SC4-WS-01 · SC4-WS-02
The Big Question
How did ancient Egyptians move 2.5-tonne stone blocks to build the Great Pyramid — using only human muscle? Scientists believe they used ramps and smooth surfaces to reduce the effort needed. Over five lessons you will investigate these ideas using the Scientific Method.
Part A — The Title
What is a title?
A concise statement naming what was studied. A good title names the independent variable (what you change) and the dependent variable (what you measure).
Title Formula
"The Effect of [Independent Variable] on [Dependent Variable]"

Practice: Write a title testing whether the mass of the block changes how far it slides.

The effect of block mass (g) on sliding distance (cm).click to check
Part B — The Aim
What is an aim?
A clear statement of what the experiment is trying to find out. Always begins with "To investigate…" or "To determine…".

Write the aim for Experiment 1 (number of books → distance block slides):

To investigate the effect of the number of books on the sliding distance (cm).click to check
Lesson 1 continued
Planning the Investigation
Page 2
Part C — The Hypothesis
What is a hypothesis?
A testable prediction that uses scientific reasoning to explain the expected outcome.

Write your hypothesis for Experiment 1 (Books) using the If/then/because structure:

If
the number of books increases (1, 2, 3 books)
then
the distance the block travels will increase
because
a steeper ramp gives gravity more influence, increasing speed.

Write your hypothesis for Experiment 2 (Surface Type):

If
the surface changes from smooth to rough
then
the distance traveled will decrease
because
rough surfaces create more friction, slowing the block.
Part D — Variables

Identify the variables for Experiment 1 (books under ramp → distance block slides): (Click box to reveal)

Independent
Variable (IV)
Number of books (1, 2, 3)
Dependent
Variable (DV)
Distance block travels (cm)
Controlled
Variables (CV)
Block mass, ramp length, starting position
Lesson 1 continued
Planning the Investigation
Page 3
Part E — Risk Assessment

Identify potential hazards, assess the risk, and plan a prevention strategy. (Click table cells to check)

HazardRisk (who is harmed and how)Prevention
Books falling off desk Books fall on foot causing injury Stack against wall, don't lean
Block sliding into student Block hits student foot Stand to side, use stopper
Sandpaper causing abrasion Sandpaper scratches skin Handle by edges
Ramp board tipping over Ramp falls and breaks or hits student Place on flat, stable desk
Part F — Materials List
Equipment List — Both Experiments
  • 1 × wooden ramp board
  • 1 × wooden block (~200g)
  • 3 × identical textbooks
  • 1 × wood surface
  • 1 × plastic sheet
  • 1 × piece of sandpaper
  • 1 × tape measure
  • Masking tape & pencil

Add two more items you think may be useful:

Stopper block, calculatorclick to check
Lesson 2 of 5 · 50 Minutes
Writing Methods
Setup Diagram & Practice Write-up
SC4-WS-03
Method Writing Rules
  • Each step is numbered
  • Each step begins with a verb (e.g. Set up, Place, Release, Measure)
  • Be specific — include measurements and quantities
  • State how many trials
Part A — Setup Diagram: Experiment 1
Upload Experiment 1 Setup Diagram

Draw and upload a diagram showing the setup for testing how books affect sliding distance.

Part B — Practice: Method Cloze

Scenario: Investigate how drop height affects tennis ball bounce. (Click lines for answers)

WORD BANK:    Measure   |   Repeat   |   Drop   |   Record   |   50 cm
1. Tape a 2-metre measuring tape vertically against a flat wall.
2. Drop the tennis ball from a starting height of 50 cm.
3. Get down to eye level and Measure the maximum height on its first bounce.
4. Record the bounce height in the results table.
5. Repeat steps 2-4 four more times.
Lesson 2 continued
Writing Methods - Practice Exercises
Page 2
Part C — Practice Exercises

Test your understanding of how to write a good scientific method.

Exercise 1: Fix the Method Step

Poorly written step: "Put the block on the ramp and let it go to see how far it goes."

Why is this bad? It lacks an action verb at the start, isn't specific, and doesn't mention units.

Rewrite this step correctly: (Click to check)

Place block at top of ramp and release without pushing. Measure distance (cm).click to check
Exercise 2: True or False

Click the boxes to reveal the correct answer.

1. A method should be written as a long, descriptive paragraph. False (Use numbered steps)
2. You must list exact amounts, like "50mL" instead of "some water". True
3. Doing 1 trial is enough to prove your hypothesis. False (Do 3-5 trials)
Exercise 3: Multiple Choice

Which of the following is the best starting word for a method step?

  • A) The
  • B) Next
  • C) Measure
  • D) I
Answer:
C) Measure (it's a verb)
Lesson 2 continued
Writing Methods
Page 3
Part D — Full Method: Experiment 1

Write the method for Experiment 1 (Books vs. Distance). Start each step with a verb and specify quantities. (Click lines to check)

1. Set up the ramp by placing the wooden board on the desk with one end supported by 1 book.

2.

Place masking tape at ramp base as start line.click to check

3.

Put block at start mark on ramp.click to check

4.

Release block without pushing.click to check

5.

Measure distance (cm) from tape to block.click to check

6.

Record distance. Repeat for 5 trials.click to check

7.

Add 2nd book and repeat steps 3-6.click to check

8.

Add 3rd book and repeat steps 3-6. Calculate means.click to check
Lesson 3 of 5 · 50 Minutes
Conducting Experiment 1
Cloze Method · Results · Graph · Discussion
SC4-WS-04
Part A — Conducting Experiment 1 (Fill-in-the-Blanks)

Complete the method outline before running the experiment. (Click lines for answers)

WORD BANK:    1 book   |   masking tape   |   same starting mark   |   Release   |   push/force   |   distance   |   5   |   2 books   |   3 books
1. Set up the ramp using 1 book to support one end.
2. Place a strip of masking tape on the floor at the base to mark the start.
3. Position the wooden block at the same starting mark at the top of the ramp for every trial.
4. Release the block gently. Do not apply any push/force.
5. Measure the distance (in cm) from the tape mark to the stopped block.
6. Repeat this process for a total of 5 trials for the first condition.
7. Change the height to 2 books and then 3 books, repeating the trials.
Part B — Results: Experiment 1
Mean = Sum of all valid trial values ÷ number of valid trials
Outlier = A value significantly off from the rest. Exclude it from the mean calculation.

Write your data table title (click to check):

Table 1: Effect of number of books on mean sliding distance (cm).click to check
Number
of Books		 Trial 1
(cm)		Trial 2
(cm)		Trial 3
(cm)		 Trial 4
(cm)		Trial 5
(cm)		 Outlier? Mean
(cm)
1 book 232524 2425 None24.2
2 books 444645 4468 Trial 544.8
3 books 707271 7270 None71.0

Click any cell to see a sample value.

Show your mean calculation for the 2-books condition (excluding any outlier):

179 ÷ 4 = 44.8 cm. Trial 5 excluded (pushed).click to check

Part C — Graph: Experiment 1

Construct a column graph of your mean results. Use a ruler.

Graph Title (click to check):

Figure 1: Mean distance (cm) for each number of books
Y-axis label → click
Distance (cm)
X-axis label (click to check): Number of Books
Part D — Discussion: Experiment 1

1. Describe the trend in your results. What happened to the distance as the number of books increased?

Distance increased from 24.2cm to 71.0cm. Steeper ramp = longer distance.click to check

2. Was there an outlier in your results? Which trial was it, and why might it have occurred?

Trial 5 (68cm) was an outlier. The block was likely pushed.click to check
Lesson 4 of 5 · 50 Minutes
Conducting Experiment 2
Setup · Cloze Method · Results · Graph
SC4-FOR-01 · SC4-WS-04
Part A — Setup Diagram: Experiment 2
Upload Experiment 2 Setup Diagram

Draw and upload a diagram showing the setup testing different surface types.

Part B — Conducting Experiment 2 (Fill-in-the-Blanks)

Complete the method outline for testing how surface type affects distance. (Click lines to check)

WORD BANK:    2 books   |   fixed/constant   |   wood   |   release   |   distance   |   plastic   |   sandpaper
1. Set up the ramp with 2 books supporting one end. This height must remain fixed/constant.
2. Place the wood surface panel on top of the ramp board.
3. Position the block at the starting mark and release it gently.
4. Measure the distance (cm) traveled along the floor.
5. Repeat for 5 trials. Then replace surface with plastic, and finally sandpaper.
Part C — Results: Experiment 2

Write your data table title (click to check):

Table 2: Effect of surface type on mean sliding distance (cm).click to check
Surface
Type		 Trial 1
(cm)		Trial 2
(cm)		Trial 3
(cm)		 Trial 4
(cm)		Trial 5
(cm)		 Outlier? Mean
(cm)
Wood 434544 4344 None43.8
Plastic 586059 5859 None58.8
Sandpaper 222423 2361 Trial 523.0

Part D — Graph: Experiment 2

Construct a column graph of your mean results.

Graph Title (click to check):

Figure 2: Mean distance (cm) for each surface type
Y-axis label → click
Distance (cm)
X-axis label (click to check): Surface Type
Part E — Discussion: Experiment 2

1. Describe the trend. Which surface produced the longest distance? Which produced the shortest?

Plastic longest (58.8cm), sandpaper shortest (23.0cm). Smooth = less friction = more speed.click to check

2. Use the word friction to explain why sandpaper produced the shortest distance.

Sandpaper grips the block, creating high friction force that slows it down.click to check
Lesson 5 of 5 · 50 Minutes
Analysing the Model
Reliability · Accuracy · Validity · Conclusions
SC4-WS-05 · SC4-WS-06
Evaluating the Results
Today you evaluate the Reliability, Accuracy, and Validity of our data.
Part A — Reliability, Accuracy & Validity: Definitions
RELIABILITY
Are results consistent?

Compare your 5 trials.
Close together = reliable.
ACCURACY
Close to true value?

Check technique: same release, exact ruler angle.
VALIDITY
Testing the right thing?

Was only the IV changed?
CV kept same?
Part B — Evaluating Reliability & Accuracy

1. Reliability: Calculate the range of trial values for Experiment 2 (Max − Min). A small range = reliable. (Click to check)

ConditionHighest trialLowest trialRange (Max − Min)Reliable?
Wood 4543 2Yes
Plastic 6058 2Yes
Sandpaper 61 (outlier)22 2 (w/o outlier)Yes (after outlier removed)

2. Accuracy: Identify one source of error, and how to fix it.

Possible Source of ErrorHow it affects resultsHow to fix it
Accidental push when releasing Overestimates distance Standardise release method
Part C — Evaluating Validity

Identify ONE controlled variable in Experiment 2 that was critical to keep valid, and explain why.

Ramp height (2 books) kept fixed so only surface type caused distance changes.click to check
Lesson 5 continued
Analysing the Model - Practice Exercises
Page 2
Part D — Practice Scenarios

Test your understanding of Reliability, Accuracy, and Validity. (Click lines to check)

Scenario 1

John ran his experiment and recorded the distances: 12cm, 13cm, 12cm, 12cm. Are his results reliable? Why or why not?

Yes, they are consistent and close together.click to check
Scenario 2

Sarah wanted to test surface friction. She used 1 book for the plastic surface and 3 books for the sandpaper surface. Is her test valid? Why or why not?

No, she changed two variables at once (surface and height).click to check
Scenario 3

Tom measured his distance using a tape measure that was stretched out, making all his readings 2cm longer than they should be in real life. Does this affect his Reliability or his Accuracy?

Accuracy (equipment error affects closeness to true value).click to check
Exercise: Quick Check

Match the term to the definition.

1. Doing lots of trials to check consistency. Reliability
2. Using proper equipment carefully to get the true value. Accuracy
3. Controlling all variables to ensure a fair test. Validity
Lesson 5 continued
Writing Conclusions
Page 3
Conclusion Formula
1. Restate aim"In this experiment, I investigated the effect of…"
2. Main finding"The results showed that…" + include data
3. Hypothesis"My hypothesis was supported / not supported because…"
4. Link to scienceRefer to friction, gravity, or kinetic energy
Part E — Write Your Conclusion: Experiment 1

(Click lines to check answers)

1. Restate the aim:
I investigated how number of books affects sliding distance.click to check
2. State the main finding with data:
Distance increased from 24.2cm (1 book) to 71.0cm (3 books).click to check
3. Was your hypothesis supported?
Supported. More books = steeper ramp = more gravity effect.click to check
4. Link to science:
Steeper angle increases acceleration and kinetic energy.click to check
Part F — Write Your Conclusion: Experiment 2
1. Restate the aim:
I investigated how surface type affects distance.click to check
2. State the main finding with data:
Plastic was longest (58.8cm) and sandpaper shortest (23.0cm).click to check
3. Was your hypothesis supported?
Supported. Rougher surfaces produced shorter distances.click to check
4. Link to science / Egyptian Context:
Smooth surfaces reduce friction, needing less effort to move blocks.click to check
★ Preparation for Lesson 6 — Assessment Task
You will receive a new data set. You must independently:
  • Calculate means and identify outliers
  • Construct a labelled column graph
  • Write a discussion addressing reliability, accuracy and validity
  • Write a full conclusion using the 4-part formula
